local_names.romanized_local: Nán Guā Zǐ (Chinese); Biji Labu (Malay); Pūcaṇi Vitaikaḷ (Tamil); Kaddū ke Bīj (Hindi)

region_of_origin: Mesoamerica (Cucurbita pepo native origin); widely consumed globally including Singapore
food_subgroup: Dried pumpkin seed kernels (hull-free); richest plant zinc source; high magnesium; Cucurbita pepo

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for raw hull-free pumpkin seed kernels. Closest parent concept: squash or pumpkin seed food product. URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pumpkin and squash seed kernels, dried (Cucurbita pepo)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0SPS is the closest FoodEx2 code for dried pumpkin/squash seed kernels; exact hull-free raw pepita sub-entry not separately coded in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw pumpkin seed kernels as a food item in available SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical clinical serving of raw pumpkin seeds is approximately one small handful (28g), providing around 157 kcal, 8.5g protein, 13.7g fat, and 2.1mg zinc.

nutrient_source.title: Seeds, pumpkin and squash seed kernels, dried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #12014
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy Foods Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC #12014 is the canonical reference for raw dried pumpkin seed kernels (Cucurbita pepo). All per-100g values are sourced directly from this entry. Confidence: high.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of pumpkin seeds — estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous low-GI seed foods; net digestible carbohydrate ~4.7g/100g (10.7g total carb minus 6.0g fibre)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); supported by Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw pumpkin seed kernels is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~25 is a defensible analogous estimate based on: (1) net digestible carbohydrate of ~4.7g/100g (10.7g total minus 6.0g fibre); (2) predominant macronutrient profile is fat (49.1g) and protein (30.2g), both of which blunt postprandial glucose response; (3) analogous low-GI seed foods (sunflower seeds, sesame seeds) in the Sydney GI Database consistently score GI 20–35. GL per 28g serving = (25 × 4.7 × 0.28) / 100 ≈ 0.33; the 0.8 figure represents GL per 100g reference portion. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (7mg/100g); high potassium (809mg/100g) and magnesium (592mg/100g) providing antihypertensive mineral support
hypertension.guidance: Raw unsalted pumpkin seeds are highly suitable for hypertensive patients: sodium content is negligible at 7mg/100g, while the potassium-to-sodium ratio exceeds 115:1, and magnesium at 592mg/100g supports vascular smooth muscle relaxation and endothelial function; a 28g serving contributes approximately 227mg potassium and 166mg magnesium toward daily antihypertensive mineral targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~25), negligible glycemic load, high magnesium (592mg/100g) critical for insulin receptor function, zinc supporting insulin synthesis
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pumpkin seeds are well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management: GI ~25 with a glycemic load of less than 1 per 28g serving minimises postprandial glucose excursion; magnesium at 592mg/100g is critically important for insulin receptor tyrosine kinase activity, and magnesium deficiency is both a consequence and an independent driver of T2DM progression; zinc (7.5mg/100g; ~2.1mg per 28g serving) supports pancreatic beta-cell insulin synthesis and secretion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable fatty acid profile (predominantly unsaturated fats: ~37.2g/100g mono+polyunsaturated); high phytosterol content; negligible sodium; high magnesium and potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pumpkin seeds support cardiovascular health through a predominantly unsaturated fat profile (monounsaturated ~16.2g and polyunsaturated ~21.0g per 100g), negligible trans fat (0g), and significant phytosterol content which may modestly reduce LDL cholesterol; the high magnesium (592mg/100g) and potassium (809mg/100g) content further supports cardiac rhythm regulation and vascular tone, making raw unsalted pumpkin seeds a cardioprotective snack choice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High zinc (7.5mg/100g) supporting immune function; magnesium supporting anti-inflammatory enzyme activity; non-haem iron addressing RA-associated anaemia
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pumpkin seeds are suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients: zinc at 7.5mg/100g (2.1mg per 28g serving) supports immune regulation and wound healing relevant for patients on biologics; magnesium (73mg per 28g) supports anti-inflammatory enzyme function; non-haem iron (~2.5mg per 28g) helps address the anaemia of chronic inflammation commonly seen in RA.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pumpkin seeds (pepitas) are the hull-free, dried inner kernels of Cucurbita pepo, the common culinary pumpkin. They are light green in colour with a firm, crunchy texture and a mild, nutty flavour. The shell-free 'pepita' form — derived from hull-less pumpkin cultivars — is the standard commercial variety widely available at Singapore supermarkets including NTUC FairPrice, Cold Storage, and Mustafa Centre. Per 100g (raw, unsalted): 559 kcal, 30.2g protein, 49.1g fat (predominantly unsaturated), 10.7g carbohydrate, 6.0g dietary fibre. Sodium is negligible at 7mg/100g. Pumpkin seeds are the richest plant-based zinc source at 7.5mg/100g, and contain an exceptionally high magnesium content of 592mg/100g — among the highest of any commonly consumed food. GI ~25 (low), glycemic load negligible. Clinically relevant: magnesium at this concentration is critical for insulin receptor tyrosine kinase activity and T2DM management; zinc supports pancreatic beta-cell function and immune regulation; potassium (809mg/100g) and the high potassium-to-sodium ratio support antihypertensive dietary patterns. Pumpkin seed extract (standardised) holds EMA-approved traditional herbal medicine status for lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC #12014. Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: are pumpkin seeds good for diabetes

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC #12014 (Seeds, pumpkin and squash seed kernels, dried), an approved primary source with exact match to the food described. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology with documented basis. Confidence: high.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0mg) — pumpkin seeds are a plant food and naturally contain no cholesterol. Vitamin A (0.0 mcg RAE), vitamin D (0.0 mcg), and vitamin B12 (0.0 mcg) are confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy #12014; these vitamins are naturally absent or negligible in plant seed kernels. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy #12014. Monounsaturated fat (16.24g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (20.98g) values sourced from USDA SR Legacy #12014 fatty acid breakdown. Water content (5.23g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy #12014. GI value of ~25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for raw pumpkin seed kernels exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GL per 28g serving calculated as (25 × 4.7 × 0.28) / 100 ≈ 0.33; the 0.8 figure in gi_source represents GL per 100g reference portion. Allergen note: pumpkin seeds are not a recognised major allergen but may be processed in facilities handling tree nuts; cross-contact risk noted. FoodOn URI not assigned — no exact FoodOn entry identified for raw hull-free pumpkin seed kernels; closest parent concept used. SNOMED CT code not assigned — no food-item SNOMED CT code identified. Hindi local name (कद्दू के बीज, Kaddū ke Bīj) added to satisfy Singapore multilingual coverage requirements for global health foods widely consumed across Singapore's multicultural population.
